speaker: interviewer
text: It's September 28, 1995. We're interviewing Helen Rosenblatt, birth name Diament. The interviewer is Martha Frazer. We're in Riverdale, New York, in the United States, and the language of the interview is English.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: We had gotten to Czechoslovakia after the war was over. So then what happened from Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Czechs were very good to us. They took good care and brought us up to the border with Austria. Why there? Because this was a place where a lot of Polish workers returned from work home, and the American were supplying their transport. So they figured out that since we want to go into Austria and Germany, we can use the trucks that the military, the MPs, the Americans were providing. And so it happened. We spent a short while in Czechoslovakia, and then we are next to Linz, on the border between Czechoslovakia and Austria. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In Linz were a lot of other Jewish people already, and organizations from the United States were already supplying food and helping them if they were sick. We stood in this place. It was a big castle, Goering's castle. And from there, we waited again until a new transport of tracks will come to bring us further up. And it happened so. They brought us to a place in Germany, where they said they cannot go any further. They put us between the coal in a wagon that we be not seen, and we came to [INAUDIBLE], a place. When we walked out from between the coals, we were all dirty and black. We could not recognize each other. Anyhow, from there we traveled again, in the vicinity of [INAUDIBLE]. We heard already that a lot of survivors are registered there. There is a list of survivors.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we rushed there to find our family. I heard only that my husband's brother survived in Buchenwald, because my husband did not survive Buchenwald. He was sent to Buchenwald. And when Buchenwald was liberated, they took all the people and shot them on the way. How did you find this out?

speaker: interviewee
text: From my brother-in-law, who survived in a hospital in Buchenwald. They didn't have time to take him with them, and he told me. So we were going further to look. Maybe in another camp there would be a list. Until we came to Frankfurt. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In Frankfurt, one of the rabbis from the United States was preparing-- Rabbi Neuhaus-- a list of survivors. And to this same place came my husband, who is now my husband, but at this time, he was from Czestochowa, and I knew him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he do before he came to Frankfurt? He also is a survivor of the Holocaust-- however, in a different way. He went under a false name of Wladystaw Pikuba. He went as a Pole to a factory in Austria, located in Bregenz am Boldensee. This is close to the Swiss border. And they made the-- bouillons were produced over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a Pole. Nobody knew that he was a Jew, because he would not survive. And he had a hard time. He could not go take a bath. He could not reveal anything from what happened before. He was like one of them, and came Christmas, he was sitting at the head of the table and making all the preparations for the holiday, as if he is one of them. He had a very hard time there. He worked in the factory, and one day, somebody came in and looked behind the heads and figured out, this head looks like a Jewish head. But he only laughed at what remarks they were making. Until one day, another Pole who worked with him, cleaning out the big, big pots that they made the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, 'You a Jew!'

speaker: interviewee
text: And he told him-- and he said to him, where are my tools? And he pushed him away and said to him, you Jew. My husband then took a big ladle and hit him over the head. This was his reaction.

speaker: interviewer
text: The meister came in and said, what happened here? He spoke German. And my husband made believe he doesn't understand. Polish, Polish, Polish, Polish. And he warned if this happens again, he would be sent to a concentration camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Until one day, shortly before the liberation. Somebody spread news that he said something about the end of the war, so right away he was put in jail, on the way to a bad concentration camp. He was very fortunate there, except for being hungry, except for being dirty. He had a guy who worked for the Germans, and he put his paper always on the bottom, to win time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he spent the three weeks in the jail, until the French army eliminated Bregenz, opened up the gates, and he was free. He didn't know then that Jews survived, but he knew that he was a Jew who survived. So he wanted to find out.

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of them tried to swim to a river to Switzerland, but they didn't let them in. So he walked-- and the French said, no, you cannot. So he started to travel, until--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Until he came to Frankfurt am Main. And he came to Rabbi Neuhaus to check the list, because he discovered that there were other Jews also. And he looked for his family. He didn't find anybody. He's one of 12 children. They all are gone. He didn't find no one. He is the only survivor of the big family. But then he heard that there was a DP camp, a displaced person camp, in Zeilsheim, not far from Frankfurt. So he and his friend came over. And in between they worked for the American soldiers in a PX, because he wanted to work and to earn money. He came to the camp, and there he found me. Now, in this camp, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was already quite a while, because I gave up looking for more family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you heard about your brother at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Not about my sister. Nothing. And to kill time and to do something positive, as a teacher I collected all the youngsters who survived and gave them a chance to complete their education. I opened up a high school for grownups. Some were 18, some were 16, some were 20, but they wanted to learn. And there were other survivors, teachers too, and we had a little school. We gave them-- they needed education that the Germans ordered to be able to go to college. So they got their diplomas, thanks to our help. And there one day, he came in, and he found me. I was the only one that he remembered from our Czestochowa. So he used to come again, and visiting and working in the PX. And it was until we were befriended, and it was until June 12, 1946 we got married. But we remained in Zeilsheim in this displaced person camp, until it came the news that everybody has to go to his destination. They worked to bring us to Israel, at this time Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this where you wanted to go, Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because in between being in the displaced person camp, I got news that my-- no, this was right after. Right. I got news that my sister survived, with her husband and with her child. And she wanted to join us, but she was in Austria through the Zionist organization, looking for ways to put people in contact with families. And we paid a smuggler, who brought them over, and then she was already in Zeilsheim, living with us for a short while. Since my brother-in-law had family in the United States, he got the priority [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] papers, and one day they left for the United States. We couldn't go because this was the time of the McCarthy era, when all those who are born behind the Iron Curtain are communists, so they are not well-seen in the United States. We cannot go. We registered to go to join my sister, but it stopped. And now a situation where I cannot live between the Germans. I cannot go to the United States. Then I go to Palestine. And we started to get organized to go to Marseilles to board a ship to go to Palestine. Our journey was not at the end at this time. A little boat that fishermen used in Panama was rented by the then-Israeli government, because they didn't have money. It was already '48, after the declaration of Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they loaded in that small boat that could take only 200 people-- they loaded a thousand. And it was very poorly equipped, and the first night, we have no motor going, and we are sinking. A big storm hit us, and overnight we sent SOS to help us.

speaker: interviewee
text: With us, it was only one representative from Jerusalem to take care of us, to bring us over. We were ready to lynch this guy if he will not help us, because they were mothers with children who survived in the forests. And they said, we survived this terrible time, and now we are going to sink in this boat. Do something. We gave an SOS, and a little, strong boat from Scandinavia with young soldiers sent back their SOS. We're going to save you. Hold on through the night, because we cannot see. The waves are so high, we cannot find you. Very shortly-- it was Christmas Eve-- when those guys threw lines over and pulled us out of the water, and brought us to the [INAUDIBLE] of Crete in Greece ready. They were very proud of what they did on Christmas Eve. We had all to sign a declaration to the king for the big deed, what they did. But we stood on Crete for another three weeks, waiting until they will repair the boat, and we have no food. They didn't let us ashore because they were afraid we were sick people. We waited again. We waited after they repaired the boat, hardly surviving, until again it started, and finally we are in Haifa. The journey from Marseilles to Haifa takes three days, we were more than three weeks on the water. There I discovered that finally my dream came true-- I am pregnant. The whole time when I was in Germany, I could not. I could not. And all of a sudden, here on this sinking boat, was a doctor from England, who understood my tragedy, my big wish to have a child, and he gave me injections. He saved me until we came to Haifa, and then straight to a hospital, because they put us in tents. And the first night, a big, strong wind came, and all in the air, and again we have no shelter. I remained in the hospital at this time. My husband went to Tel Aviv to find a quarter for us to live there. Since this was my early stage, I spent more than three months in this hospital. It was illegal. This was only for delivery and go. But the nurse covered it up and she said that I am to deliver tomorrow. She saved me again. OK, my dream came true. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In January we arrived already-- we had already a place to be in Israel, January '49. And in August the same year, my son was born, and I was so thankful. Every effort, every pain, every suffering was worth it, because he's a great person, and he is my own.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you were in Israel, you taught again. Your were a schoolteacher also in Israel. And you remained in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Not right away, because my son was born in August. So I remained with my child for the first two years. My husband found a place to live there, and it was so hard to find a place. We lived below the ground of the streets, and the name of the place was [HEBREW]. It means in Hebrew sand. There was no road. And our room was below the road, so when did the big rain season came through the window and through the door, water came in, and I had to hold onto my little baby in the window to save his life, to get out before the water will come up to here.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was very hard. Food was very scarce. We sold our rations to give him something to eat. And this was the first two years when he grew up already. And it was very hard, because he had rackets-- soft bones? Rickets, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: Rickets. Right. Thank you. And the doctor say, this child will never walk. And I was heartbroken. I went to another doctor. She was a woman, Dr. [INAUDIBLE], a very famous person. And she said--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Take this child, go to the seashore every morning, and the ultraviolet rays will put him on his feet. I carried him through the sand, very far away. Today he has two beautiful legs. I really made him strong. And after two or three years, I needed a job. Being at the dentist, who was a Polish woman, and she spoke with me, and she knew my history, and she had the feeling that I'm a real teacher. She helped me out with the headmaster of the local school. The neighborhood was Yemenite, and the headmaster came long ago from Russia. But he had a feeling that I was a good teacher, and they needed a teacher at the time. So I was assigned to a school. 60 children in one room, some disturbed children. There was no money for anything. It was the beginning. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when they first bell rang, and I had to go into my classroom, I broke down, because I was afraid that loving children and seeing what happened to the children during the Holocaust, I will have no more love for children. But I did have. I devoted my time to every child, however disturbances were around. The mothers of the children who heard that I am a newcomer, a new immigrant, so I have no right to teach the natives-- I am not good enough-- came during classes, opened up the door, and grabbed the children out. With the bell, at the recess, I went to my headmaster and cried. I cried, and he said, cry. They will change their minds. And so it happened. I was soon organizing all first-graders, this and other teachers had to get directions from me. I made a lot of friends there. I was a very successful teacher and did a very good job. And it came around 1958, we got a note from the American consulate that our application that was delivered long, long ago, after McCarthy, we can now apply. Obviously, we re-applied to go-- and my sister helped me. In Israel she sent me packages and this, but I wanted to be with family. So we were ready to go to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Meanwhile, can we just fill in what happened to your brother, and where he lives?

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. My brother survived in Mauthausen, a very famous concentration camp. He returned sick to Czestochowa to find about his family. He heard from my sister that she is there, and he heard about us too. We lived together with the rest of the Jewish fighters organization, and we got already funds from joined--

speaker: interviewee
text: What's the name of it? Do they remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] . And he didn't find me there my sister, because I was already in Germany. But then, as I told you, we smuggled her in. My brother remained there until he eventually immigrated to Israel, where he lives today.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now you got to the United States in 1958.

speaker: interviewee
text: In August 1958. The first year I looked around for a position. It was August, shortly before school opening. It was very hard. However, I taught in the afternoon school for a few hours. But my son got already into the day school. I had to bring him by bus every day, because he didn't speak a word of English. And there in this school, they showed an interest in me for a position for the coming year. At this time, I had a chance twice to substitute in Westchester Day School in Mamaroneck. And after the headmaster saw me at work, he said, I'm ready to sign with you the contract right now. I said, I cannot, because I promised the other school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one year I spent there. In 1960 I started my career in Westchester Day School as a first-grade Hebrew teacher. And you remained there until 1981.

speaker: interviewee
text: Until I retired, and I was a teacher of first grade all those 21 years. I wanted to go on with my children, who were my best friends already at the end of the school year, but my headmaster said no. You are the teacher who gives the child the right start. Because some teachers in first grade can give the child a push, and they will go on and see that learning is fun, but they can at the same time kill the spirit. So I get remained in first grade until the very end.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you always taught your first graders about the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewee
text: The first year, 1960, nobody around spoke so loudly about the Holocaust like now, and the least in schools. Nobody mentioned it. Let me now tell you why I am making this whole tape. Could you let me explain now?
